Blindfolded
Mini-Golf
in the Wind.

You are playing a game of minigolf, with a twist.
You cannot see the green! Actually you are blindfolded!

↓

The game in 30 seconds.

You are standing on the tee with a blindfold on. You choose a putting force from 0 to 100 that you will play. However, wind pushes the ball randomly. Your caddie only tells you how far you landed past or short of the hole.

Four rules to play by.

🕶️
Blindfolded playerYou cannot see the hole or the final landing point directly.
🏌️
Choose forceForce is your current guess theta_t.
🌬️
Wind randomnessLanding is force (theta_t) plus a small random offset.
📢
Signed feedbackPast is positive. Short is negative.

The score you actually get.

Unlike ordinary mini-golf, your job is not to get lucky once. Your job is to find what force will land the hole on average — intuitively, somewhere inbetween of where the wind has swayed the ball during your previous attempts.

forceYour current blindfolded guess of how hard to putt.
landingforce + wind, how the ball moves in total (never leaves the green).
errorlanding - hole. The only useful signal the caddie gives you, how far off you landed and in which direction.
goalFind the ideal force theta where the error landing - hole is approximately 0 over many attempts.

Keys to press.

Left and right adjust your force. Space putts.

Adjust force, putt, and use only the caddie's signed feedback. The reveal button is there for checking after you have played blindfolded.

Shot history
  • No shots yet.

The expected landing.

No single putt can be trusted — use the same force twice and the wind drops the ball in two different spots. So play that force many times: the wind pushes the ball forward on some putts and back on others, and those random pushes mostly cancel out. What is left is an average we call the expected landing.

Expected value

E[landing] = hole

Read E[...] as "the long-run average of." Your real target is the force whose expected landing sits exactly on the hole. And since the caddie reports the error:

error = landing - hole

finding that force is the same as driving the average error to zero — that is the Robbins-Monro problem in one line.

E[error] = 0

Robbins-Monro doesn't chase the last putt. It chases the average putt.

This is root-finding with noise.

Let theta denote the force and theta* be the correct force you would choose if the blindfold came off. The function you are trying to zero is:

Optimization problem

H(theta) = E[landing - hole] = theta - theta*

At the solution, H(theta*) = 0. The wind prevents you from seeing H exactly, so each putt gives one noisy sample.

The Robbins-Monro putting rule.

Overshot? Reduce force. Short? Increase force. But, if you add the force with a constant coefficient, you can end up oscillating between 0 and 100! This is solved by using a learning rate that shrinks over time.

One-line update

theta_(t+1) = theta_t - alpha_t * error_t

error_t = landing_t - hole. A positive error subtracts force (too strong - past the hole). A negative error adds force (too weak - short of the hole).

Implement Robbins-Monro

🎯 Your task

Complete the function robbins_monro(game, n_rounds, alpha_0, decay_rate) so that, putt after putt, the force converges on the hidden hole.

  • Given: the loop, the decaying learning rate alpha_t, the current force (game.force), and the game.putt() call are already wired up for you.
  • To do: fill in the three remaining blanks — the signed error, the change (scaled step), and the updated force. 
def robbins_monro(game: MiniGolfGame, n_rounds: int = 100, alpha_0: float = 5.0, decay_rate: float = 20.0):
    forces, signed_errors, learning_rates = [], [], []
    game.reset()

    for t in range(n_rounds):
        alpha_t = alpha_0 / (1 + t / decay_rate)
        learning_rates.append(alpha_t)

        current_force = game.force
        forces.append(current_force)

        result = game.putt(current_force)
        signed_errors.append(result['signed_error'])

        error = 
        change = 
        game.force = 

    return forces, signed_errors, learning_rates
Three blanks now. The signed error is one of the fields the caddie's result hands back — the one reporting how far past or short you landed. The change is how big a correction to make: don't move by the whole error, shrink it by the step size first. The update then applies that change to the force, moving against the error — so a past (positive) error eases off and a short (negative) error pushes harder.
🔒
Locked

Complete the code cell to continue.

The quizzes and final recap unlock after the Robbins-Monro update is correct.

Why shrink the learning rate?

Why does alpha_t get smaller over time?

Correct. Early motion needs reach; late motion needs stability. The decaying schedule averages out noisy putts.

When have you found it?

You have dialed in the perfect force. What does the caddie's signed feedback do now?

The wind never stops, so any single putt is still off. But at the ideal force the expected (long-run average) signed error is exactly 0 — that is the root you were chasing.

You found the hidden hole.

You sank an unseen, wind-blown target using only the caddie's noisy feedback — congrats!

Three ideas to carry with you:

  • Stochastic root-finding — chase a target you can only measure noisily, one putt at a time.
  • The Robbins-Monro update — theta_next = theta - alpha * signed_error.
  • Learning-rate decay — bold early, cautious late → convergence.
